one_line_takeaway: MindChat combines a dual-feedback multi-agent pipeline for synthesizing a 5.7k-session Chinese counseling dataset with a federated-learning + client-level differential-privacy + LoRA training recipe, showing better data quality and top automatic-eval scores than baselines and a modest, but non-monotonic, reduction in membership-inference and memorization risk under DP.
key_finding: MindCorpus outperforms all seven comparison datasets on nearly every quality dimension in both automatic (all 5 metrics except Trust, where ExTES scores 8.38 vs. MindCorpus's 8.32) and human evaluation (4 of 5 dimensions, all but Emotion), with moderate-to-high Spearman correlation between automatic and human scores (rho 0.659-0.819) supporting the reliability of GPT-4o-as-judge. In the multi-agent ablation, dialogues generated with no feedback loop score lowest; adding the Evaluator+Corrector loop gives a modest gain and adding the Manager loop gives a further gain, confirming both feedback loops contribute. On the CPsyCounE benchmark, MindChat achieves the highest average score in automatic evaluation (Avg 2.16) among all compared models (general-purpose and counseling-specialized), but ranks only third in human evaluation (Avg 1.75), behind DeepSeek (1.91) and Gemini (1.76) -- both far larger general-purpose models -- while still outperforming all comparably-sized general and domain-specific baselines. The data-scale/diversity study shows thematic diversity matters more than raw volume: MindChat-T2 (2 themes, less data) outperforms MindChat-Q6 (6 same-theme slices, 3x more data) on average score (2.07 vs. 2.06). On privacy, DP measurably reduces corpus memorization (ROUGE-1 recall and cosine similarity both rise monotonically as epsilon increases from 1 toward the no-DP condition) and lowers MIA ROC/PR AUC relative to the non-private baseline across all three attack types (LOSS, Min-k, Zlib) in Table VIII, but the effect size is modest -- e.g., LOSS-attack ROC AUC only drops from 0.7374 (no DP) to about 0.72-0.73 across epsilon in {1,3,5,7} -- and the paper explicitly notes epsilon=3 gives the strongest overall protection, with further tightening to epsilon=1 yielding no consistent additional improvement (privacy gains plateau and can even slightly worsen).
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions (not baselines) are threefold: (1) a dual closed-loop multi-agent role-playing framework -- six agents (Extractor, Seeker, Supporter, Evaluator, Corrector, Manager) with a turn-level critique-and-revision loop (Evaluator+Corrector) and a session-level strategy-refinement loop (Manager) -- used to synthesize MindCorpus, a 5.7k-session Chinese multi-turn counseling dialogue dataset, plus a five-dimension evaluation scheme (Professionalism, Helpfulness, Guidance, Emotion, Trust) assessing quality from both supporter and seeker perspectives; (2) a privacy-preserving, parameter-efficient distributed fine-tuning paradigm that combines federated learning (FedAvg aggregation of LoRA adapters, rank 16 / alpha 32) with client-level (epsilon, delta)-differential privacy (L2 clipping + Gaussian noise on LoRA updates) so that no institution's raw counseling data is centralized; and (3) MindChat, a Qwen3-8B-based counseling chatbot fine-tuned on MindCorpus under this framework, together with an empirical privacy evaluation of the resulting model using corpus-memorization metrics (ROUGE-1, cosine similarity) and three membership-inference attacks (LOSS, Min-k Prob, Zlib Entropy).
threat_model: The setting is federated: sensitive multi-turn counseling dialogues are held locally by distributed institutions/clients (10 clients, one per thematic category) that cannot share raw records due to privacy regulation and ethical constraints (paper cites HIPAA/GDPR-style restrictions). The adversary of interest is one capable of extracting or inferring information about individual training samples from the trained/shared model -- specifically a membership-inference adversary (LOSS-based, Min-k% probability-based, and Zlib-entropy-based attacks) that tries to distinguish member vs. non-member counseling sessions from model behavior, plus a generic memorization/regurgitation risk measured via ROUGE-1 recall and cosine similarity between generated text and training data. The paper does not model a malicious/Byzantine server or client, does not consider prompt-injection or jailbreak attacks against the deployed chatbot itself (it cites prior work on jailbreaking-induced privacy leakage from ChatGPT only as motivation, not as an attack it evaluates), and treats the server as honest-but-curious with respect to the transmitted LoRA parameter updates. Defense is client-level (epsilon, delta)-DP applied to LoRA weight updates before FedAvg aggregation.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation: the privacy mechanism operates only at the client level (protecting what leaves each institution during FL rounds); it explicitly flags that stronger guarantees would require supplementary techniques such as active forgetting or model unlearning, which it does not implement. Grounded observations from the results themselves: (1) the empirical MIA defense is modest in absolute terms -- ROC AUC only moves from ~0.74 toward ~0.72, still well above 0.5 (random guessing), so membership signal is reduced but far from eliminated; (2) tightening epsilon below 3 does not reliably improve privacy protection and can degrade it slightly, so the DP mechanism's behavior is not monotonic in the expected direction at the low end; (3) MindChat is not the top model under human evaluation -- it is beaten by two much larger general-purpose commercial models (DeepSeek, Gemini), so 'competitive with baselines' should not be read as 'best overall'; (4) MindCorpus is Chinese-only and the model is evaluated primarily on Chinese-language counseling scenarios via platforms Yixinli/Jiandanxinli, so generalization to other languages/cultures is untested; (5) automatic evaluation relies on GPT-4o as judge with only moderate (not high) correlation to human ratings on some dimensions (e.g., Professionalism and Authenticity correlations at the model level were 0.351 and 0.378, both non-significant), so some of the automatic-eval superiority claims rest on a judge whose agreement with humans is weak on exactly those dimensions.

datasets_used: MindCorpus (paper's own output): 5.7k Chinese counseling sessions, avg 12.0 utterances/session, avg length 84.0
Seed data: ~11k seeker situation texts from Yixinli and Jiandanxinli online counseling platforms, cleaned/enriched via GLM-4-Plus
Comparison emotional-dialogue datasets (baselines, not the paper's own): PsyDTCorpus, SoulChatCorpus, SMILECHAT, CPsyCounD (Chinese); ExTES, ESD-CoT, AUGESC (English)
CPsyCounE (external benchmark from prior work, used to evaluate MindChat and model baselines on Comprehensiveness/Professionalism/Authenticity/Safety)
Data-quantity/diversity study subset: 600 sessions sampled from a Depression and Anxiety theme, split into quantity slices S1-S6 and theme slices T1-T6
quantitative_results: MindCorpus vs. 7 baseline datasets, automatic eval (Table III): MindCorpus best on Pro. 8.94, Hel. 8.98, Gui. 8.96, Emo. 8.98; second on Tru. 8.32 (ExTES leads at 8.38)
MindCorpus vs. baselines, human eval (Table III): MindCorpus best on Pro. 8.45, Hel. 8.68, Gui. 8.35, Tru. 8.50; not best on Emo. (PsyDTCorpus leads at 9.12 vs. MindCorpus 8.93)
Spearman correlation (dataset-level, automatic vs. human), Table IV: Pro. rho=0.659 (p=0.076), Hel. rho=0.738 (p=0.037), Gui. rho=0.690 (p=0.058), Emo. rho=0.819 (p=0.013), Tru. rho=0.667 (p=0.071)

CPsyCounE model comparison, automatic eval Avg (Table VI): MindChat 2.16 (highest), DeepSeek 1.99, Gemini 2.00, Qwen3-8B 1.97, ChatGPT 1.86, SoulChat2.0 1.70, CPsyCounX 1.85, EmoLLM2.0 1.93, MeChat 1.90

CPsyCounE model comparison, human eval Avg (Table VI): DeepSeek 1.91 (highest), Gemini 1.76 (2nd), MindChat 1.75 (3rd), ChatGPT 1.67, Qwen3-8B 1.27, SoulChat2.0 1.36, CPsyCounX 1.28, EmoLLM2.0 0.94, MeChat 1.50
Model-level Spearman correlations (Table VI discussion): Comprehensiveness rho=0.730 (p=0.026); Professionalism rho=0.351 (p=0.354); Authenticity rho=0.378 (p=0.316)

Data quantity/diversity (Table VII), Avg score: MindChat-Local (1 client) 1.99; MindChat-Q2 1.98; MindChat-Q4 2.05; MindChat-Q6 2.06; MindChat-T2 2.07; MindChat-T4 2.09; MindChat-T6 2.13 -- T2 (2 themes) beats Q6 (6 same-theme slices, 3x the data)
MIA robustness under DP, MindChat-Q2, LOSS attack ROC AUC (Table VIII): epsilon=1: 0.7245; epsilon=3: 0.7186 (best/lowest); epsilon=5: 0.7254; epsilon=7: 0.7246; without DP: 0.7374
MIA robustness, Min-k attack ROC AUC (Table VIII): epsilon=1: 0.6945; epsilon=3: 0.6864 (best); epsilon=5: 0.6972; epsilon=7: 0.6929; without DP: 0.6920
MIA robustness, Zlib attack ROC AUC (Table VIII): epsilon=1: 0.5672; epsilon=3: 0.5646 (best); epsilon=5: 0.5675; epsilon=7: 0.5666; without DP: 0.5712
Default DP configuration used in main experiments: (epsilon, delta)-DP with epsilon=1, delta=1e-5, L2 clip norm 1
baselines_compared: Dataset-level: PsyDTCorpus, SoulChatCorpus, SMILECHAT, CPsyCounD, ExTES, ESD-CoT, AUGESC
Model-level general-purpose: ChatGPT, DeepSeek, Gemini, base Qwen3-8B
Model-level specialized counseling LLMs: SoulChat2.0, CPsyCounX, EmoLLM2.0, MeChat
Privacy baseline: non-private federated training (w/o DP) compared against epsilon in {1,3,5,7}
